evals.name: silent-failures-simple
evals.expected_output: Two silent failure patterns: (1) Task {} with try? — if fetchProfile throws, the error is silently discarded and profile stays nil forever with no log, no crash report. (2) try? erases all diagnostic information — was it a 401, a timeout, or a decoding error? All context lost. Fix: use do/catch inside the Task with Logger.error() and ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal(), and distinguish CancellationError from real errors.

assertions.text: Identifies Task {} as a silent error swallowing pattern because Task.init is @discardableResult
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific knowledge about Task {} error handling — baseline may just suggest adding do/catch without explaining why

assertions.text: Identifies try? as destroying diagnostic information (error type, message, stack context)
assertions.rationale: Skill emphasizes try? is the most dangerous convenience for observability — baseline often accepts try? as fine

assertions.text: Recommends Logger.error() with privacy annotations instead of print()
assertions.rationale: Skill mandates os.Logger over print — baseline typically suggests print(error)

assertions.text: Recommends reporting to a crash SDK (ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal or Sentry/Crashlytics)
assertions.rationale: Skill requires remote error reporting in every catch block — baseline rarely mentions crash SDKs

evals.name: silent-failures-medium
evals.prompt:
This Combine search pipeline works once but then stops responding. What's wrong?

```swift
$searchQuery
    .debounce(for: .seconds(0.3), scheduler: DispatchQueue.main)
    .flatMap { query in
        APIService.search(query)
    }
    .replaceError(with: [])
    .assign(to: &$results)
```
evals.expected_output: When APIService.search emits a failure, .replaceError(with: []) changes the publisher's Failure type to Never, which terminates the entire pipeline permanently. Error recovery must happen inside flatMap to keep the outer pipeline alive. Fix: move .catch inside flatMap, add .retry(2) before the catch, and report the error to ErrorReporter.

assertions.text: Explains that .replaceError() kills the entire pipeline permanently (changes Failure type to Never)
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly covers this critical Combine behavior — baseline may suggest a different fix

assertions.text: Recommends moving error handling inside flatMap to keep the outer pipeline alive
assertions.rationale: The correct fix from the skill — error recovery must be scoped to the inner publisher

assertions.text: Includes ErrorReporter or crash SDK call inside the error recovery
assertions.rationale: Skill requires every error path to report remotely — baseline just fixes the pipeline without observability

evals.name: silent-failures-complex
evals.expected_output: Multiple silent failure patterns: (1) loadOrders: try? hides fetch errors — user sees empty orders with no error message. (2) placeOrder: two try? calls — if submitOrder fails, sendConfirmationEmail still runs on stale data; if either fails, print() goes nowhere in production. (3) deleteOrder: Task.detached with try but no do/catch — if delete throws, error is silently discarded. (4) All three methods use print() or no logging at all. Fix all with do/catch, Logger.error() with privacy annotations, ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal(), proper error state for UI, and CancellationError handling.

assertions.text: Identifies all three methods have silent failure patterns (try? in loadOrders, try? in placeOrder, unhandled throw in deleteOrder)
assertions.rationale: Comprehensive audit requires finding all three — skill trains systematic scanning

assertions.text: Identifies that placeOrder's two try? calls create a logic error (confirmation email sent even if order submission fails)
assertions.rationale: Skill teaches that try? hides control flow bugs, not just diagnostic loss

assertions.text: Identifies print('Order placed successfully') is invisible in production (not part of unified logging system)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: print() is not error handling, not visible on production devices

assertions.text: Identifies Task.detached strips priority and cancellation propagation (rarely correct choice)
assertions.rationale: Skill cross-references with swift-concurrency knowledge about detached tasks

assertions.text: Provides fix with os.Logger using privacy annotations (not just print or generic logging)
assertions.rationale: Skill mandates privacy-annotated Logger — the key differentiator from baseline

assertions.text: Adds ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal() or crash SDK call in every catch block
assertions.rationale: Remote reporting in every error path — the core skill requirement

evals.name: logger-setup-simple
evals.prompt: I'm setting up logging for my iOS app. Currently using print() everywhere. What should I use instead and why?
evals.expected_output: Use os.Logger (import os). Create Logger extensions with subsystem (bundle identifier) and categories (Networking, Auth, Database, etc.). Logger uses binary representation with zero cost for debug messages in production, has privacy annotations for GDPR compliance, persists error/fault levels to disk, and is filterable in Xcode and Console.app. print() writes to stdout with no levels, no persistence, no privacy, and is invisible on production devices.

assertions.text: Recommends os.Logger (not OSLog, not custom logging framework, not third-party like CocoaLumberjack)
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes os.Logger as the standard — baseline may suggest alternatives

assertions.text: Shows Logger extension pattern with subsystem = Bundle.main.bundleIdentifier and named categories
assertions.rationale: Skill provides the exact setup pattern — baseline may show inline Logger creation

assertions.text: Mentions privacy annotations as a key feature (dynamic strings redacted by default in production)
assertions.rationale: The most important Logger feature that baseline responses almost never mention

assertions.text: Explains that .debug level messages have zero cost in production (compiler optimizes away)
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific knowledge about Logger performance characteristics

evals.name: logger-setup-medium
evals.prompt:
What privacy annotations should I use in these Logger calls?

```swift
logger.error("Request to \(url) failed for user \(userId) with token \(authToken)")
logger.info("Saving order \(orderId) for \(customerEmail) total \(amount)")
```
evals.expected_output: First line: url.absoluteString with .public (URL paths are safe), userId with .private(mask: .hash) (allows correlation without exposing PII), authToken should NOT be logged at all (it's a secret). Second line: orderId with .public (internal identifier), customerEmail with .private(mask: .hash) (PII), amount with .public (numeric, non-identifying). Without annotations, all interpolated strings show as <private> in production logs.

assertions.text: Identifies that authToken should not be logged at all (or uses .sensitive if logged)
assertions.rationale: Skill's PII rules: authentication tokens must never appear in logs

assertions.text: Recommends .private(mask: .hash) for userId or customerEmail (not just .private)
assertions.rationale: Skill teaches mask: .hash for correlation without PII exposure — key differentiator

assertions.text: Explains that without privacy annotations, production logs show <private> for all interpolated strings
assertions.rationale: The fundamental reason privacy annotations matter — baseline may not explain default redaction behavior

evals.name: logger-setup-complex
evals.prompt: We're a health tech app (HIPAA-adjacent). Design a logging strategy that gives us good debugging capability while staying compliant. We use SwiftUI + async/await and talk to a REST API.
evals.expected_output: Layered approach: (1) os.Logger with strict privacy annotations — all health data .private, all PII .private(mask: .hash) for correlation, URL paths .public, status codes .public. (2) ErrorReporter protocol abstracting Sentry/Crashlytics — never log PHI in crash report extras. (3) Redactor utility for manual masking in crash SDK context. (4) PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest declaring Required Reason API usage. (5) MetricKit for OOM/hang detection without PII. (6) Five log levels: .debug for dev tracing (free in production), .info for operational context, .notice for key events, .error for failures, .fault for bugs.

assertions.text: Addresses HIPAA/health data compliance specifically in the logging strategy
assertions.rationale: Skill's PII compliance reference covers health data explicitly

assertions.text: Recommends PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest (required since May 2024)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers privacy manifests as mandatory for App Store — baseline often omits this

assertions.text: Recommends a Redactor utility or @Redacted property wrapper for model-level PII protection
assertions.rationale: Enterprise pattern from the skill — prevents accidental PII exposure through string interpolation

assertions.text: Recommends MetricKit alongside crash SDK for out-of-process diagnostics
assertions.rationale: Skill teaches MetricKit as complementary to crash SDKs — baseline rarely mentions it

evals.name: crash-sdk-simple
evals.prompt: I want to add crash reporting to my iOS app. Should I use Sentry or Crashlytics? What are the trade-offs?
evals.expected_output: Sentry: richest API — structured breadcrumbs with level/category/data, scope modifications for tags/extras, automatic HTTP/lifecycle/touch breadcrumbs, session replay, performance tracing. Best for teams wanting deep observability. Crashlytics: tight Firebase integration, simpler setup, free tier. Limitation: only stores 8 non-fatal exceptions per session. Best for Firebase-heavy projects. Both: wrap behind an ErrorReporter protocol so you can swap vendors later. Never run both for fatal crashes — signal handler conflict.

assertions.text: Mentions Crashlytics limitation of 8 non-fatal exceptions per session
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific knowledge about Crashlytics limits — baseline almost never mentions this

assertions.text: Warns against running both for fatal crash reporting (signal handler conflicts)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers signal handler conflicts — critical production knowledge

assertions.text: Recommends wrapping behind a protocol/abstraction layer (ErrorReporter)
assertions.rationale: Skill's architectural pattern — enables vendor swapping and testability

evals.name: crash-sdk-medium
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) URLSession doesn't throw for HTTP 4xx/5xx — must validate statusCode explicitly. (2) No observability — errors propagate with no logging or crash reporting. Fix: check HTTPURLResponse.statusCode, add Logger calls with privacy annotations on the URL path and status code, add ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal() for non-200 responses and decode failures, add breadcrumb before the request.

assertions.text: Identifies that URLSession does not throw for HTTP 4xx/5xx (only transport errors throw)
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly covers this — one of the most commonly missed silent failure patterns

assertions.text: Adds breadcrumb before the network request
assertions.rationale: Skill teaches breadcrumbs before risky operations — baseline never adds breadcrumbs

assertions.text: Uses Logger with privacy annotations (.public for URL path and status code)
assertions.rationale: Skill mandates privacy annotations — baseline uses print() or bare string interpolation

assertions.text: Calls ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal() or SentrySDK.capture() in the error path
assertions.rationale: Remote error reporting — the skill's core requirement for every error path

evals.name: crash-sdk-complex
evals.prompt: We're using both Sentry and Crashlytics in our iOS app. Crashes sometimes don't appear in either dashboard. What's going wrong and how do we fix it?
evals.expected_output: Signal handler conflict: both SDKs install handlers for SIGABRT, SIGSEGV, SIGBUS, and only the last registered receives the signal. NSSetUncaughtExceptionHandler supports only one handler — second call replaces the first. Fix: pick one for fatal crash reporting (disable the other's crash handler), use the secondary only for non-fatals. Example: options.enableCrashHandler = false for Sentry if using Crashlytics for crashes. Also ensure dSYMs are uploaded for both SDKs, and add MetricKit for OOM/watchdog kills that neither in-process reporter catches.

assertions.text: Identifies signal handler conflict (SIGABRT, SIGSEGV, SIGBUS — only last registered handler receives the signal)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers this specific technical root cause — baseline may only say 'they conflict'

assertions.text: Identifies NSSetUncaughtExceptionHandler supports only one handler
assertions.rationale: Second mechanism that causes conflicts — skill covers this explicitly

assertions.text: Recommends disabling crash handler on one SDK (e.g., options.enableCrashHandler = false)
assertions.rationale: Specific actionable fix from the skill — baseline may just say 'remove one SDK'

assertions.text: Recommends MetricKit for crashes that in-process reporters miss (OOM, watchdog)
assertions.rationale: Skill's layered approach — MetricKit catches what neither SDK can

evals.name: enterprise-simple
evals.prompt: How should I handle errors in SwiftUI views? Right now my catch blocks just set an error string and show an alert.
evals.expected_output: Use a centralized ErrorHandler (ObservableObject) injected via environment. Define a CategorizedError protocol with categories: retryable, nonRetryable, requiresLogout. The handler routes errors: retryable triggers retry UI, nonRetryable shows user message and reports non-fatal, requiresLogout clears auth state. Every path logs to Logger and reports to ErrorReporter. This compensates for SwiftUI lacking React-style Error Boundaries.

assertions.text: Recommends centralized ErrorHandler as ObservableObject with environment injection
assertions.rationale: Skill's enterprise pattern — baseline typically handles errors per-view

assertions.text: Recommends CategorizedError protocol with retryable/nonRetryable/requiresLogout categories
assertions.rationale: Skill's error categorization pattern — baseline uses generic error handling

assertions.text: Mentions SwiftUI lacks Error Boundaries (unlike React) as motivation for centralized handling
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly calls out this SwiftUI limitation

evals.name: enterprise-medium
evals.prompt: Implement a retry mechanism for network requests that has good observability. We want exponential backoff with jitter.
evals.expected_output: Generic retryWithBackoff function: takes maxAttempts, base delay, operation name. Each attempt logged with Logger.warning (attempt number, max attempts, error description with privacy annotations). Breadcrumb added for each failed attempt. Only reports to crash SDK after ALL retries exhausted (prevents noise from transient failures). Uses exponential delay with random jitter (0 to 50% of delay) capped at 60 seconds.

assertions.text: Reports to crash SDK only after all retries are exhausted (not on each attempt)
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly teaches this — prevents noise from transient failures

assertions.text: Adds breadcrumb for each failed retry attempt
assertions.rationale: Skill's observability pattern — breadcrumbs provide retry history in crash reports

assertions.text: Uses Logger.warning (not .error) for intermediate retry failures
assertions.rationale: Skill distinguishes log levels — intermediate failures are warnings, final failure is error

assertions.text: Includes jitter in the backoff delay (not just pure exponential)
assertions.rationale: Skill includes jitter to prevent thundering herd — baseline often omits jitter

evals.name: enterprise-complex
evals.prompt: Our app has a main app, a widget extension, and a notification service extension. Crashes in the widget and notification extension aren't appearing in our Sentry dashboard. How do we fix this?
evals.expected_output: App extensions run in separate processes — they have their own memory space and lifecycle. Crash SDK must be initialized separately in each extension's entry point. Each extension target needs its own dSYM upload in build phases. For Crashlytics, each needs its own GoogleService-Info.plist. Use App Groups (UserDefaults(suiteName:)) to share user context and error configuration between main app and extensions. Disable autoSessionTracking in extensions. Also add MetricKit in each extension for OOM detection (extensions have ~50MB memory limit for widgets).

assertions.text: Identifies that app extensions run in separate processes (not the main app process)
assertions.rationale: Root cause of the problem — skill explicitly covers this commonly missed concern

assertions.text: States crash SDK must be initialized separately in each extension's entry point
assertions.rationale: The fix — skill provides the exact pattern for extension initialization

assertions.text: Mentions each extension needs its own dSYM upload configuration
assertions.rationale: Skill covers this — without dSYMs, extension crashes show hex addresses

assertions.text: Recommends App Groups for sharing error config between main app and extensions
assertions.rationale: Skill's enterprise pattern for multi-process error coordination

assertions.text: Mentions disabling autoSessionTracking in extensions
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific detail — extensions don't have normal session lifecycle

evals.name: metrickit-simple
evals.prompt: What is MetricKit and should I use it alongside Sentry?
evals.expected_output: MetricKit is Apple's first-party diagnostic system that runs out-of-process. It catches OOM terminations, watchdog kills, and background terminations that in-process crash reporters like Sentry miss entirely. Limitation: only receives data from users who opt into 'Share with App Developers'. Sentry captures from all users with rich context and breadcrumbs but misses OOM/watchdog kills. Use both: MetricKit for OS-level diagnostics, Sentry for everything else.

assertions.text: Explains MetricKit runs out-of-process (not inside the app)
assertions.rationale: Key architectural difference — this is why it catches what Sentry/Crashlytics miss

assertions.text: Identifies OOM and watchdog kills as things MetricKit catches that in-process reporters miss
assertions.rationale: Skill's primary reason for recommending MetricKit

assertions.text: Mentions the opt-in limitation ('Share with App Developers' in Settings)
assertions.rationale: Critical limitation that affects coverage — skill explicitly warns about this

assertions.text: Recommends using both MetricKit and a third-party crash SDK
assertions.rationale: Skill's layered approach — use both for comprehensive coverage

evals.name: objc-exceptions-simple
evals.prompt: Will Swift's do/catch handle all errors in my iOS app? Are there exceptions it won't catch?
evals.expected_output: No. Swift's do/catch only catches Swift Error types. Objective-C NSExceptions are NOT caught by Swift error handling — they terminate the app without Swift intervening. Common triggers: NSArray out-of-bounds, invalid KVO key paths, unrecognized selectors, Core Data threading violations. Also: Swift traps (force unwrap nil) and EXC_BAD_ACCESS bypass both Swift and ObjC error handling — only signal handlers catch these. Defense: validate inputs before calling ObjC APIs, use crash reporting SDKs that install signal handlers.

assertions.text: States that Objective-C NSExceptions are NOT caught by Swift do/catch
assertions.rationale: Fundamental gap in Swift error handling — skill covers this explicitly

assertions.text: Lists specific NSException triggers (NSArray out-of-bounds, invalid KVO, unrecognized selector)
assertions.rationale: Skill provides actionable examples — baseline may be vague

assertions.text: Mentions that Swift traps (force unwrap nil) also bypass do/catch (caught by signal handlers only)
assertions.rationale: Skill's error type coverage table — shows the full picture

evals.name: pii-compliance-simple
evals.prompt: What should I watch out for with logging and GDPR compliance in my iOS app?
evals.expected_output: GDPR classifies IP addresses, device IDs, and behavioral data as personal information. Never log: passwords, JWT tokens, email addresses, phone numbers, full names, IDFA. Also watch for operational leaks: full HTTP request/response bodies (may contain auth headers), database queries with user parameters, URL paths containing emails. Use os.Logger privacy annotations (.private for PII, .private(mask: .hash) for correlation). PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifests are required since May 2024 — apps without them are rejected. Crash reporting generally doesn't require ATT consent if data is only used for improving your app.

assertions.text: Mentions operational leaks (HTTP bodies with auth headers, DB queries with user params, URL paths with emails)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers commonly missed PII vectors — baseline only mentions obvious PII

assertions.text: Mentions PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifests required since May 2024
assertions.rationale: Skill covers this App Store requirement — baseline rarely mentions privacy manifests

assertions.text: States crash reporting generally does not require ATT consent
assertions.rationale: Skill clarifies this common confusion — crash diagnostics are not 'tracking'

assertions.text: Recommends .private(mask: .hash) for correlation without PII exposure
assertions.rationale: Skill's specific privacy annotation recommendation — baseline may just say 'don't log PII'
